title: Request a Shift Change
summary: Ask your supervisor to change your work shift politely, with enough notice and a clear reason.
learnerPromise: You will be able to ask for a shift change in a respectful way that gives your supervisor enough information and time to consider your request.
whyItMatters: Workplaces run on reliable staffing. A polite, advance request shows responsibility and makes it more likely your supervisor can accommodate you.
realLifeUses: Asking to swap a shift because of a doctor's appointment you scheduled before knowing your work schedule
Requesting a specific day off for a family event or school commitment
Asking to change from a closing shift to an opening shift due to transportation changes
Offering to cover another shift in exchange for the one you need changed
materialsNeeded: A clear reason for the request
A suggested solution or alternative shift
result: A respectful request delivered to your supervisor with a suggested alternative and a clear timeframe.
youWillLearn: How to ask for a shift change as early as possible
How to explain your reason briefly and honestly without oversharing
How to suggest a solution, such as a specific coworker who could swap or cover
How to accept the answer gracefully, whether it is yes or no
miniChallenge: Write out what you would say to request a shift change for a real or imaginary scheduling conflict. Include your reason and a suggested solution.
steps: Check your workplace policy on shift changes. Some places require a minimum notice period or a specific form.
Contact your supervisor as soon as you know about the conflict. In person is usually best, followed by a written message to confirm.
State your request clearly: which shift you need to change, why, and what date you are talking about.
Suggest a solution. For example, mention a coworker who has agreed to swap, or offer to work a different shift that week.
Thank your supervisor for considering it. If they say no, accept it politely and try to solve your conflict another way.
completionCriteria: You made your request at least a few days in advance, or as early as you possibly could
Your request included the specific shift, a brief reason, and a suggested alternative
commonProblems: Waiting until the last minute — most supervisors need time to adjust staffing
Not offering a solution — suggesting a swap or alternative makes the request easier to approve
Oversharing personal details — a brief reason like 'a family commitment' or 'a medical appointment' is enough
Getting upset if the answer is no — staffing needs are real, and your supervisor may not have flexibility
tips: Build goodwill by being flexible when your supervisor asks you to cover shifts for others
Keep a record of approved shift changes in case of payroll confusion later
If shift changes are frequent at your workplace, find a coworker you trust and agree to swap with each other when possible
Some workplaces use apps for shift swapping — learn how yours works if one is available
